Governing Law: Alabama Uniform Trust Code (Ala. Code § 19-3B-101 et seq.)
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of an inter vivos trust; may be required for instruments affecting real property to be recorded
Witnesses Required: Not required for inter vivos trusts
Special Execution Rules: A trust may be created by transfer of property, declaration by the owner, exercise of a power of appointment, or other methods under Ala. Code § 19-3B-401
Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust exists; date of execution; identity of settlor; identity of currently acting trustees; statement of revocability or irrevocability; statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Alabama follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in Ala. Code § 19-3B-1013)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Alabama recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under UTC unless limited by the trust instrument

Governing Law: Alaska Trust Act (AS 13.36.010 et seq.)
UTC Adopted: No (independent trust code with significant asset protection provisions)
Revocability Presumption: Revocable unless expressly made irrevocable, subject to statutory limitations (AS 13.36.338)
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of a trust; may be required for recordable real property instruments
Special Execution Rules: A trust may be created by transfer of property to a trustee, declaration by the owner, or other recognized methods under Alaska law
Certification Required Elements: No fixed statutory list of required elements; third parties may rely on a certification containing relevant trust provisions
Certification Permissive Elements: Trust existence; date of execution; identity of trustees; trustee powers; revocability; other provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Alaska broadly permits trust customization, including asset protection features; specific powers depend on trust terms
Statutory Form Available: Yes (statutory recognition of certification of trust under AS 13.36.200, but no mandatory form)
Registration Requirement: Registration of a trust in Alaska is permitted but not universally required; depends on administration and court involvement
Real Property Rule: Transfers involving real property must comply with Alaska recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust under Alaska law; specific standard requires further validation
Specific Authority Required for Certain: Yes, self-settled asset protection trusts must comply with specific statutory requirements (AS 34.40.110)

Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust exists; date of execution; identity of settlor; identity of currently acting trustees; statement of revocability or irrevocability; statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Arizona follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in A.R.S. § 14-11013)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Arizona recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under UTC unless limited by the trust instrument

Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Arkansas follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in Ark. Code § 28-73-1013)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Arkansas recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under UTC unless limited by the trust instrument

Governing Law: California Probate Code §§ 15000–18201
Revocability Presumption: Revocable unless expressly made irrevocable, subject to the scope limitation in Prob. Code § 15400
Notarization Required: Not required for trust validity by the Probate Code sections reviewed; acknowledged declaration is required for a certification of trust, and acknowledgment may be needed for recordable real property instruments
Witnesses Required: Not required by statute for inter vivos trusts in the Probate Code sections reviewed
Special Execution Rules: A trust may be created by declaration, transfer during lifetime, transfer by will or other instrument taking effect at death, exercise of a power of appointment, or an enforceable promise to create a trust (Prob. Code § 15200). A trust in relation to real property is not valid unless evidenced by one of the methods in Prob. Code § 15206.
Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in any manner that would make the certification incorrect; statement that it is signed by all currently acting trustees; acknowledged declaration signed by all currently acting trustees
Certification Permissive Elements: Trust existence and date of execution; identity of settlor or settlors and currently acting trustees; trustee powers; revocability or irrevocability and identity of any person holding a power to revoke; signature authority of multiple trustees; trust identification number; manner in which title to trust assets should be taken; legal description of trust real property
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — do not rely on current field without manual legal review
Statutory Form Available: Yes (Prob. Code § 18100.5 provides statutory certification content/form requirements)
Real Property Rule: For a trust in relation to real property, validity requires one of the evidentiary methods in Prob. Code § 15206; a certification of trust relating to real property may be recorded, but recording is not required by § 18100.5
Competency Requirement: UNCLEAR — requires separate official-source validation
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — current phrasing overstates what has been confirmed from official California sources reviewed here

Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Colorado follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in C.R.S. § 15-5-1013)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Colorado recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under UTC unless limited by the trust instrument

Governing Law: Connecticut Uniform Trust Code (C.G.S. § 45a-499a et seq.)
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of an inter vivos trust; may be required for instruments affecting real property to be recorded
Witnesses Required: Not required for inter vivos trusts
Special Execution Rules: A trust may be created by transfer of property, declaration by the owner, exercise of a power of appointment, or other methods recognized under C.G.S. § 45a-499dd
Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust exists; date of execution; identity of settlor; identity of currently acting trustees; statement of revocability or irrevocability; statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Connecticut follows the Uniform Trust Code, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in C.G.S. § 45a-499tt)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Connecticut recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under the Uniform Trust Code unless limited by the trust instrument

Governing Law: Delaware Trust Act (12 Del. C. § 3301 et seq.)
UTC Adopted: No (Delaware has its own comprehensive and highly flexible trust code)
Revocability Presumption: Revocable unless expressly stated otherwise; however, Delaware strongly defers to the terms of the trust instrument
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of a trust; may be required for instruments affecting real property to be recorded
Special Execution Rules: A trust may be created by transfer of property, declaration by the owner, exercise of a power of appointment, or other recognized methods under Delaware law; Delaware law emphasizes maximum freedom of disposition and enforceability of governing instrument terms
Certification Required Elements: No mandatory statutory list; a certification or excerpt must contain information reasonably necessary to establish trustee authority and allow third-party reliance
Certification Permissive Elements: Trust existence; date of execution; identity of trustees; trustee powers; revocability; authority to act; other provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Delaware permits extensive modification and expansion of trustee powers; trustee authority is primarily governed by the terms of the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (Delaware recognizes certification of trust under 12 Del. C. § 3344, but does not mandate a specific form)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Delaware recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to transfer property
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — Delaware allows broad customization of trustee powers, including directed trusts, decanting, and other advanced structures, subject to the governing instrument

Governing Law: D.C. Uniform Trust Code (D.C. Code § 19-1301.01 et seq.)
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of an inter vivos trust; may be required for instruments affecting real property to be recorded
Witnesses Required: Not required for inter vivos trusts
Special Execution Rules: A trust may be created by transfer of property, declaration by the owner, exercise of a power of appointment, or other methods under D.C. Code § 19-1304.01
Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust exists; date of execution; identity of settlor; identity of currently acting trustees; statement of revocability or irrevocability; statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — District of Columbia follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in D.C. Code § 19-1310.13)
Registration Requirement: No general trust registration requirement; court involvement occurs only as invoked (D.C. Code § 19-1302.01)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with District of Columbia recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under UTC unless limited by the trust instrument

Governing Law: Florida Trust Code (Fla. Stat. § 736.0101 et seq.)
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of an inter vivos trust; however, a certification of trust must be acknowledged, and notarization may be required for recordable real property instruments
Witnesses Required: Not required for all inter vivos trusts; however, if a revocable trust has testamentary aspects, execution must comply with will formalities (two witnesses) under Fla. Stat. § 736.0403(2)(b)
Special Execution Rules: Testamentary aspects of a revocable trust by a Florida domiciliary must be executed with the formalities required for wills under Fla. Stat. § 736.0403(2)(b)
Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust exists; date of execution; identity of settlor; identity of currently acting trustees; statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; revocability or irrevocability; identity of any person holding power to revoke; manner in which title to trust property should be taken; trust tax identification number; other administrative provisions
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — requires jurisdiction-specific legal review (homestead and fiduciary limitations may apply)
Statutory Form Available: Yes (statutory certification structure provided in Fla. Stat. § 736.1017)
Registration Requirement: No general trust registration requirement; trust is not subject to continuing judicial supervision unless ordered by a court (Fla. Stat. § 736.0201(3))
Real Property Rule: Transfers involving Florida homestead property are subject to constitutional restrictions; additional execution and spousal joinder requirements may apply
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, equivalent to testamentary capacity under Fla. Stat. § 736.0402
Specific Authority Required for Certain: Yes, particularly regarding homestead property and certain fiduciary actions

Governing Law: Georgia Trust Act (O.C.G.A. § 53-12-1 et seq.)
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of an inter vivos trust; may be required for instruments affecting real property to be recorded
Witnesses Required: Not required for inter vivos trusts
Special Execution Rules: A trust is created by a written instrument signed by the settlor and requires intent, identifiable trust property, and a beneficiary, in accordance with O.C.G.A. § 53-12-20 et seq.
Certification Required Elements: No fixed statutory list of required elements; an affidavit of trust may be used to establish trustee authority for third parties
Certification Permissive Elements: Trust existence; identity of trustees; trustee powers; authority to act; other provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Georgia provides statutory and implied trustee powers; specific limitations depend on the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: No
Real Property Rule: Affidavit of trust may be used in real property transactions to establish trustee authority; recording requirements follow Georgia real property law
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers depend on statutory provisions and the trust instrument

Governing Law: Hawaii Uniform Trust Code (HRS § 560:7-101 et seq.)
Notarization Required: Not required
Certification Required Elements: Trust existence, date, trustees, relevant powers, revocability
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: Power to sell or encumber real property
Statutory Form Available: No
Registration Requirement: Trustee shall register trust with court (HRS § 560:7-101)
Competency Requirement: Capacity to transfer property
Specific Authority Required for Certain: Yes, for real property transactions

Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are generally defined by the trust instrument and Indiana statute; no universal statutory list requiring express grant

Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to transfer property
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — depends on trust terms and fiduciary duties under Indiana law

Governing Law: Iowa Trust Code (Iowa Code § 633A.1101 et seq.)
Notarization Required: Yes, acknowledgment before a notary public is required for creation of an inter vivos trust (Iowa Code § 633A.2102)
Special Execution Rules: An inter vivos trust must be in writing, signed by the settlor, and acknowledged before a notary public under Iowa Code § 633A.2102
Certification Required Elements: Trust existence; date of execution; identity of currently acting trustees; powers of the trustee relevant to the transaction; statement of revocability or irrevocability; statement that the trust has not been modified or terminated in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Identity of settlor; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are generally governed by the trust instrument and Iowa statute; no universal statutory list requiring express grant
Statutory Form Available: No (statutory certification framework exists but no mandatory form)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Iowa recording laws; acknowledged instruments are required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — depends on trust terms and fiduciary duties under Iowa law

Governing Law: Louisiana Trust Code (La. R.S. 9:1721 et seq.)
UTC Adopted: No (civil law jurisdiction with unique trust code)
Revocability Presumption: Irrevocable unless expressly stated otherwise (La. R.S. 9:2021)
Notarization Required: Trust must be executed either by authentic act (notary and two witnesses) or by act under private signature duly acknowledged before a notary (La. R.S. 9:1752)
Witnesses Required: Two witnesses required if executed as an authentic act; not required if executed as an acknowledged private act
Special Execution Rules: Trust must be executed by authentic act or by act under private signature duly acknowledged; strict civil law formalities apply to trust creation (La. R.S. 9:1752)
Trust Certification Statutory Basis: No direct equivalent to UTC-style certification of trust; third parties typically rely on the full trust instrument or certified excerpts
Certification Required Elements: Not specifically defined by statute
Certification Permissive Elements: Relevant excerpts of the trust instrument, including trustee identity and authority
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: Trustee powers are more constrained under Louisiana law and are primarily governed by the trust instrument and statute; careful drafting is required
Statutory Form Available: No
Pour-Over Will Recognized: Yes, subject to Louisiana succession law requirements
Real Property Rule: Transfers involving immovable property must comply with Louisiana recording laws; authentic act or duly acknowledged act is generally required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to donate inter vivos under Louisiana civil law
Specific Authority Required for Certain: Yes — Louisiana requires explicit drafting of trustee powers, and actions not clearly authorized in the trust instrument may be restricted

Governing Law: Maine Uniform Trust Code (18-B M.R.S. § 101 et seq.)
Notarization Required: Not required for validity of an inter vivos trust; may be required for instruments affecting real property to be recorded

Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust exists; date of execution; identity of settlor; identity of currently acting trustees; statement of revocability or irrevocability; statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Maine follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in 18-B M.R.S. § 1013)
Registration Requirement: Trust registration with the court is permitted but not generally required; applicable primarily in specific administrative or judicial contexts (18-B M.R.S. § 701)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Maine recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under UTC unless limited by the trust instrument

Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; trust identification number; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Minnesota follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in Minn. Stat. § 501C.1013)
Registration Requirement: Trust registration with the court is permitted but not generally required; applicable in limited administrative or judicial contexts (Minn. Stat. § 501C.0701)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Minnesota recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted unless limited by the trust instrument

Governing Law: New York Estates, Powers and Trusts Law (EPTL) §§ 7-1.1 et seq.
UTC Adopted: No (New York has its own long-standing trust law)
Notarization Required: An inter vivos trust must be executed and acknowledged in the manner required for recording a conveyance of real property, unless executed in the presence of two witnesses who sign the instrument (EPTL § 7-1.17)
Witnesses Required: Two witnesses are required only if the trust is executed under the witness alternative in EPTL § 7-1.17 rather than by acknowledgment
Special Execution Rules: Every lifetime trust must be in writing and executed and acknowledged by the settlor and, unless the settlor is the sole trustee, by at least one trustee, in the manner required for recording a conveyance of real property, or instead executed in the presence of two witnesses who affix their signatures (EPTL § 7-1.17)
Trust Certification Statutory Basis: UNCLEAR — no enacted New York trust-certification statute comparable to UTC § 1013 was identified in the official EPTL sources reviewed
Certification Required Elements: UNCLEAR — not specifically defined by statute in the official New York sources reviewed
Certification Permissive Elements: Relevant excerpts or attorney/trustee confirmation of trust existence, trustee identity, and trustee authority may be used in practice, but official statutory content requirements are unclear from the sources reviewed
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — New York fiduciaries have broad statutory default powers under EPTL § 11-1.1, but self-dealing and similar conflicts remain subject to fiduciary-law limits and trust-specific drafting
Statutory Form Available: No specific statutory certification form identified in the official sources reviewed
Registration Requirement: No general trust registration requirement identified in the official sources reviewed
Real Property Rule: For lifetime trusts, execution/acknowledgment must satisfy EPTL § 7-1.17; recordable real property instruments involving the trust must also satisfy New York recording requirements
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create the trust; exact standard should be validated carefully against New York trust-specific authority before production use
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — New York provides broad fiduciary default powers, but trust-specific drafting remains important for conflict-prone or specially structured powers

Governing Law: R.I. Gen. Laws Title 18 (including § 18-4-27 and Chapter 18-14); R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-4-27
UTC Adopted: No (Rhode Island has its own trust statutes)
Revocability Presumption: UNCLEAR — official Rhode Island sources reviewed confirm that a written trust is not invalid merely because the settlor retains a power to revoke, amend, alter, or modify it, but no general default presumption was identified
Signature Required: UNCLEAR — official Rhode Island sources reviewed establish that the trust must be created by a written instrument, but no general standalone signature rule was identified from the statutes reviewed
Notarization Required: Not required for general trust validity in the official Rhode Island sources reviewed; acknowledgment may be required for recordable real property instruments and for a recorded affidavit or memorandum of trust in real-property transactions
Witnesses Required: UNCLEAR — no general witness requirement for trust validity was identified in the official Rhode Island sources reviewed
Special Execution Rules: A trust is valid under R.I. Gen. Laws § 18-4-27 if it is otherwise valid and created by a written instrument; real-property trust transactions may use a recorded affidavit or memorandum of trust under R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-4-27
Trust Certification Statutory Basis: R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-4-27 (affidavit or memorandum of trust for real-property transactions)
Certification Required Elements: For a recorded affidavit or memorandum of trust in the real-property context: trust name if any; date of trust instrument; name of each settlor; name and address of each current trustee; statement regarding trustee authority to convey, mortgage, lease, grant restrictions, easements, or other interests in real estate, with relevant trust provisions attached; statement whether anything in the trust derogates from those powers
Certification Permissive Elements: Other information contained in an affidavit or memorandum of trust that is relevant to the real-property transaction
Certification Prohibited Elements: UNCLEAR — no general statutory prohibition list was identified in the official Rhode Island sources reviewed
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — no general Rhode Island statute reviewed established a statewide list of trustee powers requiring express grant
Statutory Form Available: No mandatory form identified; Rhode Island statutes authorize an affidavit or memorandum of trust for real-property transactions
Registration Requirement: No general trust registration requirement identified in the official Rhode Island sources reviewed
Real Property Rule: Property to be held in trust shall be conveyed to the trustees of the trust, and any transfer or mortgage of trust property by the trustees requires recording of the trust instrument as amended/restated or, alternatively, the affidavit or memorandum of trust under R.I. Gen. Laws § 34-4-27
Competency Requirement: UNCLEAR — no general Rhode Island trust-capacity standard was identified in the official statutes reviewed
Specific Authority Required for Certain: For real-property transactions, the affidavit or memorandum of trust must state the trustee's authority to convey, mortgage, lease, grant restrictions or easements, or other interests in real estate, and whether anything in the trust limits that authority

Certification Required Elements: Statement that the trust exists; date of execution; identity of settlor; identity of currently acting trustees; statement of revocability or irrevocability; statement that the trust has not been revoked, modified, or amended in a manner that would make the certification incorrect
Certification Permissive Elements: Trustee powers; authority of co-trustees; manner in which title to trust property may be taken; other administrative provisions relevant to the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Vermont follows UTC, which grants broad default trustee powers unless limited by the trust instrument
Statutory Form Available: Yes (certification of trust structure provided in 14A V.S.A. § 1013)
Real Property Rule: Transfers of real property involving a trust must comply with Vermont recording laws; acknowledgment or notarization may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity to create a trust, generally equivalent to capacity to make a will
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — trustee powers are broadly granted under Vermont law unless limited by the trust instrument

Governing Law: Washington Trust Act (RCW 11.98) and related Washington probate and trust statutes
UTC Adopted: No (Washington has its own trust statutes, UTC-influenced)
Revocability Presumption: UNCLEAR — no blanket UTC-style default presumption was confirmed from the official Washington trust sources reviewed here; revocability depends on trust terms and applicable Washington law
Notarization Required: Not required for general trust validity from the Washington trust sources reviewed here; may be required for instruments affecting real property to be recorded
Witnesses Required: Not required for general trust validity from the Washington trust sources reviewed here
Special Execution Rules: Washington trust creation and administration are governed by Washington-specific trust statutes rather than the UTC; real-property related trust instruments must satisfy applicable conveyancing and recording requirements
Certification Required Elements: A certification of trust may state the existence of the trust and the date of execution; the identity of the settlor; the identity and mailing address of each currently acting trustee; the powers of the trustee relevant to the pending transaction; the revocability or irrevocability of the trust and the identity of any person holding a power to revoke the trust; the authority of cotrustees to sign or otherwise authenticate and whether all or less than all are required; and the name of the trust or the titling of trust property
Certification Permissive Elements: Other information relevant to the transaction that is permitted by RCW 11.98.075
Certification Prohibited Elements: Trust dispositive provisions are not required unless necessary for the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — no general Washington statutory list of trustee powers requiring express grant was confirmed from the official sources reviewed here
Statutory Form Available: Yes (Washington provides statutory certification content in RCW 11.98.075, but not a mandatory fill-in-the-blank form)
Pour-Over Will Recognized: Yes, under Washington probate and wills law
Registration Requirement: No general trust registration requirement; a trustee may register a trust as a Washington trust under RCW 11.98.005
Real Property Rule: Real-property trust instruments must comply with Washington conveyancing and recording requirements; acknowledgment may be required for recordation
Competency Requirement: UNCLEAR — exact Washington trust-capacity standard should be validated from official Washington authority before production use
Specific Authority Required for Certain: UNCLEAR — Washington trustee powers and limitations should be reviewed under Washington-specific trust statutes and the trust instrument

Governing Law: Puerto Rico Trust Act (Act No. 219-2012, as amended)
UTC Adopted: No (civil law jurisdiction with its own trust statute)
Notarization Required: Yes, an inter vivos trust must be constituted by public deed; real-property trust instruments must also satisfy recording requirements
Witnesses Required: UNCLEAR — no general two-witness rule was confirmed from the official trust statute materials reviewed here; public-deed execution follows Puerto Rico notarial law
Special Execution Rules: An inter vivos trust must be created by public deed; real property held in trust must be recorded in the Property Registry in the name of the trust
Trust Certification Statutory Basis: No specific UTC-style certification of trust statute identified in the official Puerto Rico trust materials reviewed
Certification Required Elements: UNCLEAR — not specifically defined by statute in the official Puerto Rico trust materials reviewed
Certification Permissive Elements: Relevant notarized excerpts or certified copies of the trust instrument may be used to establish trustee authority, depending on the transaction
Non-Default Powers Requiring Express Aut: UNCLEAR — Puerto Rico is a civil law jurisdiction and trustee powers should be reviewed carefully against the trust instrument and applicable Puerto Rico law
Statutory Form Available: No specific statutory certification form identified
Pour-Over Will Recognized: UNCLEAR — requires separate Puerto Rico succession-law validation before production use
Registration Requirement: No general court registration requirement identified, but real property held in trust must be recorded in the Property Registry in the name of the trust
Real Property Rule: Real property held in trust must be recorded in the Property Registry under the name of the trust; instruments affecting trust real property must satisfy Puerto Rico deed and recording requirements
Competency Requirement: Settlor must have capacity under Puerto Rico civil law; exact trust-capacity standard requires separate validation
Specific Authority Required for Certain: Yes — real-property transactions and other trustee acts should be confirmed through the trust instrument and Puerto Rico civil-law formalities
